episodes.title: The financial scams that brought Albania to the brink of war
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Historical FT podcast on Albania's 1990s pyramid-scheme collapse: how post-communist financial vacuum, IMF Washington Consensus shock therapy, and regulatory capture enabled Ponzi schemes to absorb roughly the equivalent of Albania's entire GDP, culminating in ~2,000 deaths and near civil war in 1997. The episode's macro relevance lies in its structural lessons on shadow banking, IMF conditionality, and the dynamics of unregulated high-yield financial innovation — with explicit modern parallels drawn to crypto and shadow banking sectors.

**Key points**
- Three dysfunctional state banks post-1990 created a credit vacuum filled by unregulated "investment firms"; some originated as legitimate businesses (hotels, supermarkets), others were outright criminal/smuggling enterprises
- By mid-1996, Vefa alone held $120m in deposits (~5% of GDP); at peak, two-thirds of Albanians had exposure; total liabilities approached full GDP
- Schemes offered 47% monthly interest at the extreme; government, IMF, and World Bank characterised Albania as a Washington Consensus success story as late as 1996
- IMF 1999 post-mortem admitted treating schemes as positive contributors to growth — a rare institutional self-indictment; shock therapy left no regulatory or supervisory infrastructure
- Collapse triggered army/police desertion, mass looting of Soviet-era armories, ~2,000 deaths; Italy-led UN peacekeeping force required; IMF/World Bank bailout conditioned on zero depositor compensation
- Episode draws explicit parallel to crypto: unregulated high-yield promises, political donation-driven regulatory capture in the US, and risk of legitimate actors being swept up with fraudulent ones in a crisis

**FX / rates implications**
- Shadow banking read-through for EM: the Albania dynamic — regulated banks dysfunctional, unregulated NBFI fills the void, systemic risk builds invisibly — maps loosely onto China NBFI stress, Vietnam property credit, and crypto-adjacent capital flows in EM
- IMF conditionality mechanics (no depositor bailout as prerequisite for programme lending) remain a live template for EM sovereign restructuring negotiations

**Watch list**
- US crypto regulatory evolution (post-2024 election cycle) and spillover into EM capital flows and dollar-substitute demand
- China NBFI / shadow credit stress as structural analogue to the Albania vacuum dynamic

episodes.title: "But China!": Robert Wright on the AI Race and Our Coming Cosmic Reckoning
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
A lengthy philosophical conversation between Kaiser Kuo (Sinica Podcast) and author Robert Wright on the geopolitical and existential stakes of the US-China AI race, framed through Wright's new book *The God Test*. The episode is primarily intellectual — evolutionary biology, cognitive empathy, global governance — but the US-China tech-decoupling thread runs throughout and has weak, indirect read-throughs for EM traders via CNY and broader risk sentiment.

**Key points**
- Wright argues US chip export controls on China have backfired on their own terms: supply constraints forced Chinese labs toward efficiency innovations, producing DeepSeek, and effectively accelerated Chinese domestic semiconductor development rather than curtailing it.
- The "But China" argument — invoking Beijing as justification for blocking AI regulation, safety review processes, and international governance — is characterised as a convenient talking point for Silicon Valley capital, with Trump reportedly rescinding a frontier-model pre-release review EO after lobbying from his AI adviser David Sacks.
- Anthropic's geopolitical positioning (Dario Amodei co-authoring a WSJ op-ed with Matt Pottinger calling for US chip dominance over China) is discussed critically; Wright argues the race framing and sprint dynamic are precisely what make careful AI evaluation impossible.
- DeepSeek's open-weight model strategy is framed as a deliberate response to chip restrictions — building a global developer ecosystem where closed US frontier models cannot easily compete.
- No economic data, FX levels, rates, or central bank content discussed.

**FX / rates implications**
- The chip-control-backfire narrative, if it gains policy traction in Washington, marginally reduces the tail risk of further semiconductor export escalation — a modest positive for CNY/CNH and Taiwan-linked supply-chain sentiment (TWD).
- Sustained US-China AI rivalry and the "arms race" framing remain a structural headwind for risk sentiment in EM Asia; any escalation toward data-centre sabotage scenarios flagged by Wright would be a sharp risk-off catalyst.

**Watch list**
- Any reversal or softening of US chip export controls toward China as a policy signal.
- Follow-on AI governance dialogue between Washington and Beijing (Wright notes a recent summit produced a commitment to at least begin talks).

episodes.title: The Week That Was - 15 Jun: Inflation Data in, Markets Rise
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
India's May CPI printed at 3.93% YoY (vs 3.48% in April), while FY26 GDP surprised to the upside at 7.7% (vs 7.1% FY25) — a constructive macro mix for INR but with inflation trending higher and the RBI flagging a 5.1% average for the year ahead. The dominant weekly catalyst was geopolitics: US-Iran peace deal optimism drove crude ~6% lower, directly fueling INR's sharpest single-day gain in weeks and a broad equity recovery.

**Key points**
- India CPI May: 3.93% YoY (Apr: 3.48%); food inflation 4.78%; fuel prices rose for the first time in four years; rural inflation (4.25%) outpacing urban (3.53%)
- RBI projects inflation averaging ~5.1% for the coming year, explicitly flagging Middle East energy-cost risk
- India FY26 GDP: 7.7% (FY25: 7.1%), beat; GFCF growth at 10.8% in Q4 signals a firm capex cycle; services resilient, manufacturing slowed to 7.3% in Q4
- US CPI May: headline 4.2% YoY (3-year high), driven by energy/gas from Middle East conflict; core CPI softer MoM — mixed signal for the Fed
- ECB delivered its first hike since 2023 (+25bps), revised inflation forecasts higher, citing Middle East energy pressures
- Brent/WTI fell ~6% on the week to ~$87/bbl, extending below $83 on Monday on Iran deal optimism
- Nifty +1.1% on the week (Nifty Bank +4%), NASDAQ swung -2% then +2.5% around the US CPI print; IT sector lagged on AI disruption concerns

**FX / rates implications**
- INR: crude oil decline is the primary near-term driver — lower oil compresses India's import bill and CAD, USDINR moved to ~85.11 on Friday and extended gains to ~84.7 on Monday; further oil downside would sustain INR bid
- RBI's 5.1% inflation forecast and the uptick in food/fuel components push back near-term rate-cut expectations; any re-acceleration in CPI could flip the RBI more hawkish
- US CPI at 4.2% (3-year high) keeps the Fed on hold or hawkish-biased — a headwind for EM carry broadly; core softness is the sole dovish offset and may not be enough to shift Fed pricing materially
- A confirmed Iran peace deal on 19 June would sustain crude downside — structurally bullish for oil-importing EM FX: INR, IDR, PHP all benefit through the same CAD/terms-of-trade channel

**Watch list**
- US-Iran peace deal signing targeted for 19 June — binary outcome for crude and EM risk sentiment
- Brent: watch $83/bbl; break lower would extend oil-importer EM FX gains
- RBI guidance: next meeting — how the MPC frames the food/fuel inflation uptick against the strong GDP print will set rate-cut timeline expectations
- Fed reaction to 4.2% CPI: watch for hawkish repricing in UST/DXY that could cap EM FX gains
- India FPI flows: strong GDP print may attract debt/equity inflows — monitor weekly NSDL data

episodes.title: Brexit: Is that it? – Live from London
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Reuters Econ World marks Brexit's 10th anniversary with a live panel examining the UK's economic and political fallout, revisiting the gilt market's vulnerability to UK political risk and Labour's nascent EU reset agenda. For EM traders, the episode is most relevant through the GBP/gilt volatility channel and the broader European political risk backdrop that weighs on EUR — both key drivers of global risk sentiment and DM FX positioning.

**Key points**
- UK official forecast: Brexit has cost 4% of productivity and 15% of trade flow (imports/exports); a US think tank estimates 6-8% of GDP lost, contested but directionally consistent.
- Lowest 40% of UK earners have seen real incomes decline over the past decade — structural demand weakness.
- City of London employment is at record highs; job losses far below feared 100k, but billions in banking assets and EU-linked trading pipework have relocated.
- UK gilt market is described as structurally more fragile post-Brexit: no ECB backstop, elevated political uncertainty (six prime ministers since 2016), Liz Truss mini-budget cited as a live template for bond vigilante action — gilts sold off on a single parliamentary appearance by Chancellor Reeves.
- Labour pursuing an EU "reset": youth mobility/experience scheme, veterinary/SPS food agreement, and energy market linkage; customs union alignment discussed as a longer-term possibility.
- EU charging a premium for partial access (£500m/yr for Erasmus re-entry vs prior £200-300m); defence loan programme priced at €6bn — UK declined.
- Political risk: Reform/Farage leads UK polls; Burnham and Streeting potential Labour leadership contenders with varying pro-EU rhetoric; possible by-election within weeks.
- RN in France and AfD polling ahead in Germany flagged as risks to EU coherence over the next decade.

**FX / rates implications**
- GBP: Structural political instability and bond vigilante risk keep a risk premium in UK rates; any renewed Labour leadership contest or fiscal slippage is a GBP and gilt negative. EU reset progress is a modest GBP positive via reduced trade friction.
- EUR/GBP: Closer UK-EU alignment (customs union trajectory) narrows the Brexit discount in EUR/GBP; political fragmentation risk in France/Germany caps EUR upside.
- UK Gilts: No ECB backstop means gilt spreads over Bunds remain sensitive to UK political shocks — a Truss-style event would ripple into EM via global risk-off and USD strength.
- Broad risk sentiment: European political instability (France, Germany, UK) is a slow-burn negative for risk appetite with modest EM carry implications.

**Watch list**
- Labour Party by-election result and leadership challenge timeline — a contest reopens gilt volatility risk.
- UK-EU summit outcomes on youth mobility/SPS deal — catalyst for GBP and EUR/GBP.
- French RN and German AfD poll trajectory ahead of respective elections — EU fragmentation risk gauge.
- UK Gilt 10y spread vs Bunds — early warning for bond vigilante re-engagement.

episodes.title: How the US-Iran Deal Could Affect Oil Prices
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Goldman Sachs commodity research (Don Ströven, co-head Global Commodities) lays out the post-US-Iran deal oil outlook, with Brent having sold off from >$120 to ~$80 as the market prices a base-case Strait of Hormuz reopening by end-July. For EM FX/rates traders the read-through is two-sided: relief for Asian importers on inflation and current-account dynamics, but still ~$20/bbl above pre-war levels with asymmetric upside risk.

**Key points**
- Brent ~$80 currently; GS forecasts $80 end-2026, $75 in 2027; WTI $75/$70 respectively
- Conflict caused ~14% loss of global oil production; global demand down ~5% (~5 mbpd)
- Base case: Strait of Hormuz flows recover to ~70% of normal by end of July; MOU to be signed in Switzerland on Friday — ship counts watched from that date
- Risk skew asymmetric: upside scenario Brent >$130 (gradual Gulf recovery, Strait stays closed); downside scenario Brent $60 in 2027 (fast reopening, persistent demand losses, strong non-Middle-East supply); probabilities roughly equal but upside move ($50+) dwarfs downside ($20)
- China crude imports down 4-5 mbpd YoY — the single biggest reason prices are not triple digits; coal switching, power substitution, and surging EV sales absorbing the shock
- Inventories still in ~5% deficit; structural disruption risk premium expected to persist even in benign scenarios
- ~90% of 5 mbpd demand losses expected to recover by 2027; ~0.5 mbpd structural demand loss from accelerated EV adoption

**FX / rates implications**
- INR, IDR, PHP, KRW, THB: $75-80 Brent still materially above pre-war ~$60 — current accounts remain under structural pressure vs pre-war; limits central bank easing room via imported inflation; upside scenario ($130+) would be a severe negative catalyst for Asian importer FX
- MXN, BRL, COP: base case $80 Brent supportive for fiscal revenues and FX; downside at $60 in 2027 would pressure EM oil-exporter sovereign dynamics and currencies; upside scenario strongly positive for these names
- Persistent oil risk premium keeps global inflation elevated, complicating EM easing cycles broadly
- China's structural demand destruction (EV, coal) is long-run disinflationary for Asian EM importers regardless of Hormuz outcome

**Watch list**
- Friday: MOU signing in Switzerland and first ship transits through Strait of Hormuz — primary near-term binary
- Weekly ship counts through Hormuz from Friday onward (70% of normal = GS base case trigger)
- Upcoming Iran-US nuclear talks — key tail risk to deal durability and upside oil scenario
- Brent key levels: $80 base / $130 upside scenario / $60 downside scenario
- Monthly China crude import data — structural demand-destruction signal

episodes.title: China's Economy Is Stronger and Weaker Than You Think
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
China's structural divide between resilient industrial output and collapsing domestic demand is widening sharply — May exports surged 19% YoY and the monthly trade surplus hit $105bn (5-month cumulative $452bn, annualizing toward the 2023 record ~$1.2trn) while retail sales fell 0.6% YoY (first decline since Dec 2022) and fixed asset investment contracted 4.1%. For EM traders this matters because China's overcapacity export push is flooding global markets and compressing trade balances across Asia and LATAM, while a consumer-weak China constrains commodity demand — a double hit on resource-exporter currencies.

**Key points**
- May retail sales -0.6% YoY, first negative since Dec 2022; home prices accelerating lower; FAI -4.1% YTD vs expectations
- Industrial production +4.5% in May (vs +4.1% April), above forecasts — supply-demand imbalance driving the export surge
- May trade surplus $105bn; 5-month cumulative $452bn, on track to match last year's record ~$1.2trn full-year surplus
- Xi resisting expansion of social safety net despite post-COVID adviser recommendations; household savings rate 35–40% of income structurally depresses consumption
- Xi's crackdown on tech giants (Alibaba, DiDi) and explicit pivot from growth to equality seen compressing long-run productivity; GDP trajectory risks drifting to 2–3% vs prior 5–6% trend
- China diversifying trade toward Global South (Brazil, Indonesia, Nigeria) as US/EU decouple; Trump-Xi May summit yielded only 200 Boeing orders vs expected 500 — read as deliberate signal of optionality toward Airbus/Europe
- Structural fragilities flagged: demographics shrinkage, property downturn, post-COVID consumer confidence shock from zero-COVID policy rigidity

**FX / rates implications**
- CNY/CNH: record current-account surplus structurally supports CNY but weak domestic demand and property overhang keep PBoC on easing bias; watch fixing for any managed depreciation signal if capital outflows resume
- CLP, BRL, COP, PEN: China's weak FAI and consumer demand are headwinds for copper, iron ore and agricultural commodity prices — CLP most directly exposed via copper, BRL via soy/iron ore; near-term China trade diversion to LATAM is a flow positive but does not offset structural demand weakness
- IDR, MYR, VND: competing as export hubs against Chinese overcapacity in manufactured goods; VND and MYR most exposed to margin compression and trade diversion risk
- Broad risk channel: China slowdown + US-China trade friction sustains risk-off bias for high-beta EM across Asia and LATAM; carry trades in high-yielders (BRL, IDR, PHP) vulnerable to China-driven commodity shock

**Watch list**
- China June retail sales, FAI and CPI/PPI prints — confirm or deny trend; deflationary PPI spills into EM import prices
- PBoC daily CNY fixing and any reserve requirement or rate moves signalling stimulus pivot
- Copper and iron ore spot as leading indicators of China demand trajectory (CLP, BRL real-time proxy)
- US-China trade talks: whether Boeing symbolic order signals broader de-escalation or sets a ceiling on détente
